certificate.moduleName: Motorola Solutions Advanced Crypto Engine (MACE) HSM – Security Level 3
certificate.caveat: When installed, initialized and configured as specified in Section 11 of the Security Policy. No assurance of the minimum strength of generated SSPs
certificate.description: The MACE is a single-chip cryptographic module to meet FIPS 140-3 Level-3 physical security requirements. The Module provides encryption and decryption services for secure key management, and secure voice/data traffic for Motorola Solutions Micro HSM product.

Key Agreement Scheme provides 192 bits of encryption strength

128 and 256-bit AES-KW keys used for encryption of keys in key transport operation and for enabling secure communication with target devices. Key establishment methodology provides 128 or 256 bits of encryption strength
256-bit AES KW (SP 800-38F) key used to decrypt the external seed.
Key establishment methodology provides 256 bits strength
Key establishment methodology provides 256 bits strength.
[IG 2.4.A] P25 AES OTAR. No Security Claimed. AES MAC is used as part of OTAR but is considered obfuscation. KTS encryption is performed on the OTAR key components using AES KW Cert. #A2527.

Module HW version, version information, and FIPS status.

The password requires a minimum of 1 Upper case, 1 Lower case, 1 Numerical and 1 special character. Since the minimum password length is 8 ASCII printable characters and there are 95 ASCII printable characters, the probability of a successful random attempt is 1 in {(10)*(262)*(32)*(954)} which is 1 in 17,619,399,200,000 The Module has a default setting of 15 consecutive failed attempts. The module will have to be reinitialized after the 15 consecutive failed attempts. Within a one minute period a successful random attempt is 15 in 17,619,399,200,000

The password requires a minimum of 1 Upper case, 1 Lower case, 1 Numerical and 1 special character. Since the minimum password length is 8 ASCII printable characters and there are 95 ASCII printable characters, the probability of a successful random attempt is 1 in {(10)*(262)*(32)*(954)} which is 1 in 17,619,399,200,000. The Module has a default setting of 15 consecutive failed attempts. The module will have to be reinitialized after the 15 consecutive failed attempts. Within a one minute period a successful random attempt is 15 in 17,619,399,200,000.

Covered with a hard-opaque epoxy coating that provides evidence of attempts to tamper with the MACE.
Look for signs of tampering. Remove from service if tampering found.
Shutdown - A tamper flag is raised, a wake- up reset of the product is triggered.
Shutdown - A tamper flag is raised, a wake- up reset of the product is triggered.
Shutdown - A general reset of the chip is asserted.
Shutdown- A tamper flag is raised, a wake- up reset of the product is triggered.
Generated external to the Module and installed during manufacturing.
Derived from the DRBG input per SP800-90Ar1.
FIPS 186-4 compliant ECDSA key generation, using the internal CAVP validated DRBG.
CKG - Symmetric key generated by internal CAVP validated DRBG.
EC Diffie-Hellman shared secret generation using the internal CAVP validated 56Arev3 protocol.
Generated per SP800-133r2 (Section 6.3 #2) via XOR of 2 other keys (IDK-ROM and IDK-Block)
Stored in the flash in plaintext, associated by memory location (pointer).
Stored in the flash in encrypted, associated by memory location (pointer).
Input electronically using SP800-38F AES key transport by the DSEK using AES KW Cert. # A2528.
Input electronically AES-256 CFB-8 encrypted on the PEK using AES KTS Cert. #A5273.
Input or output electronically using SP800-38F AES key transport on the KEK or TEK using AES KW Cert. #A2527.
Input electronically in plaintext as part of protocol.
Output electronically in plaintext public key.
Zeroized by the “Program Update” service by overwriting with a fixed pattern 0s.*
Zeroized by module power cycle or hard reset by overwriting with a fixed pattern 0s.*

Externally generated, a minimum of 48 bytes are passively entered into the Module by the User.
CTR_DRBG internal state: V (128 bits) and Key (AES 256).
256-bit AES KW (SP 800-38F) key used to decrypt the external seed.
A 256-bit AES key used for decrypting Load entropy into the MACE.
A 256-bit AES CBC key used in the re- constructed of IDK per SP800-133r2 (Section 6.3 #2) via XOR using IDK-Block
A 256-bit AES CBC key used in the re- construction of IDK per SP800-133r2 (Section 6.3 #2) via XOR using IDK-ROM
A 256-bit AES CBC key used to decrypt downloaded firmware images.
256-bit AES-CFB8 key used for decrypting passwords during password validation.
256-bit AES CFB-8 key used to encrypt all TEKs and KEKs stored in the flash.
128 and 256-bit AES- KW keys used for encryption of keys in key transport operation.
128 and 256-bit AES keys used for enabling secure communication with target devices.
384-bit password hash stored in the non-volatile memory.
SRTP/SRTCP Master Key. 128 or 256-bit key used in SRTP KDF.
SRTP/SRTCP Master Salt. 112-bit key used in SRTP KDF, or 96- bit key to generate IV internally for AES GCM encryption operation.
TLS KDF Master Secret. 384-bit secret key material.
CVL TLS KDF Cert. #A2535 or CVL SRTP KDF Cert. #A2534, AES-GCM Cert. #A5275 and #A5274
KDF Derived Key. Keys derived using TLS or SRTP KDFs.
The Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (DH) private key used for establishing a shared secret over an insecure channel.
The Elliptic Diffie- Hellman (DH) Shared Secret (SS) is established as a part of DH key agreement scheme.
384-bit ECDSA Private Key used to generate the signature of the input data from the Generate Signature service request.
2048-bit RSA key used to validate the FW Loading before it is allowed to be executed. Note - FW-LD-Pub is also used during FW integrity to validate the signature of the firmware image
The Elliptic Curve (EC) Diffie-Hellman (DH) public key, used for establishing a
shared secret over an insecure channel.
The Elliptic Curve (EC) Diffie-Hellman (DH) public key for the other party, used for establishing a shared secret over an insecure channel.
ECDSA Public key used to validate the signature of the input data from a service request.
The Load Entropy service provides the security strength required for the random number generation mechanism Per IG 9.3.A, Example 2A, there is no assurance of the minimum strength of generated SSPs.

The Module enters the critical error state. In this state, the Module stores the status into the internal flash memory and then halts all further operation by entering an infinite loop. The operator may correct this state by power cycling the Module.
The Module fails a firmware loading during program upgrade and/or firmware integrity pre- operational self-test.

The Module enters the firmware signature validation failure state. In this state, the Module halts all further operations by entering the flash programming mode. The operator may correct the issue by power cycle and/or re- flashing a new image.
The MACE fails an ECDSA PCT.
The MACE enters a temporary error state. The generated key is not used, and the Module returns an error code (0x1) to the operator. The key is discarded, and the process abandoned.
A digital signature is generated over the Boot Block, Base
firmware and all Drop-in algorithms code when it is built using
stored with the code upon download into the MACE. When
the MACE is powered up, the digital signature is verified. If the
digital signature matches, then the test passes, otherwise it

A digital signature is generated over the code when it is built using SHA2-256 and RSA-2048. The digital signature is verified upon download into the Module.
